---
title: "Prospect Dev Salary Snapshot 2025"
author: Greg Brooks
date: 04/25/2026
draft: true
categories:
    - Industry Commentary
---

Salary research for prospect dev roles curated from LinkedIn, public databases, and more.

***

![](cash2.jpg){width=50%}

# Overview
This is exactly what it sounds like: a little snapshot of industry data, independently curated. I'm not trying to compete with the [Apra International Salary Survey](https://aprahome.org/Resources/Salary-Survey){target="_blank"}, and I doubt any of my findings will be all that surprising to those who have read any of the 100+ pages of that report.

#### The Good News
The data I have gathered is free to explore and download - instead of being locked down in a PDF. Also, I don't have the 3.5% margin of error the Apra Salary Survey does, because I'm avoiding self-report data entirely, except as a means for comparison.

#### The Bad News
If you want stronger methodology, more comprehensive data (e.g. demographics), broader scope, and mild abuse of the "3d" effect for bar charts...you'll have to wait until the next Apra Salary Survey comes out in 2027.[^paywall] I limited myself to public information, which as any prospect researcher will tell you, can sometimes be prone to massive gaps.

# The Prospect Dev Salary Dataset
The first attempt at cobbling this dataset together yielded:

- 1507 individuals, with varying levels of missing information, but that can be enriched over time
- 152 jobs tied to individuals, with salary information from 2017-2025
- 84 job listings, between 2024-2026, including descriptions, some salary information, and number of applicants as of the date researched

The job listings dataset will be analyzed and published later, though I'm including some of the salary highlights in this post.

I focused on public university employees, since their salaries are easy to find via government websites.

# Initial Data

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

pn.extension('tabulator')

# read job salary CSV
salary_df = pd.read_csv("higher_ed_prospect_dev_salary_data.csv")

# format table
table = pn.widgets.Tabulator(
    salary_df, 
    pagination='local', 
    page_size=20, 
    selectable=True)

# render table
table  # this will render inline


```

***
# Additional Methodology

## Salary Sourcing & Pre-processing

### Bulk Data
I made a data request to the National Labor Exchange (NLx) Research Hub, to try and get access to a larger corpus of online job adds, occupation codes, tools, salaries, etc. However, I don't think my research case is as compelling as the guys at Loyola who trained an [entire model](https://github.com/Job-Ad-Research-at-QSB-LUC/JAAT) on billions of data points to make very slick job analysis modules in Python. Luckily, their Job Ad Analysis Toolkit will help me with my unstructured text problem - which has been building up as I grab job descriptions and other data from LinkedIn. However, their giant corpus, as far as I know, is under an agreement with NLx that may specify what they can or can't publish. 

I need the job-level data so I can filter for prospect dev roles, and I'm tempted to email them to ask for a subset of their corpus, but again, the NLx agreement may prevent that.

National bulk data for prospect dev roles remains out of reach, for now, so I had to do a state-by-state approach, starting with large public universities.

### Large Public Universities
- 
- Started with the biggest public universities in every state, then went to the open data portal for bulk downloads of salary data. Found name of university foundation or development department, filter


***

# My reading list to learn more
- [Performance-Based Salary Distribution in Universities Using Extreme Gradient Boosting with Particle Swarm Optimization](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/abstract/document/11341436)
- [Education vs. Earnings: An Analytical Perspective on Fields of Study and Salaries Using Python](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-95652-2_31)
- [Extracting O*NET Features from the NLx Corpus to Build Public Use Aggregate Labor Market Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.01470)

***

# References & Sources
For a full list of sources, see (this file)[] on the repo

- OpenGovPay.com
- GovSalaries.com
- Regional Apra chapter job postings
- Indeed
- Fiscal salary schedules from annual reports & supplements (PDFs usually)
- Bulk downloads from places like the Indiana Gateway for Government Units, Utah Open Data, etc
- LinkedIn Job Alerts
- Subhani, S., Memon, S.A. & AlShebli, B. CMap: a database for mapping job titles, sector specialization, and promotions across 24 sectors. Sci Data 12, 1214 (2025). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-025-05526-3


[^paywall]: I'm also curious who actually pays the $275 non-member fee to access the salary survey...which seems too steep to be realistic as an actual product people would buy. I'm guessing that number was chosen to make the Professional Membership benefits seem all that more attractive, a [psychological pricing technique](https://www.advergize.com/glossary/psychological-pricing/){target="_blank"} that nudges users toward the ongoing subscription. Presumably, though, there are some folks in prospect dev who don't have access to the Professional Membership, which is another reason to have independent and free data for all.
